coredata.dc:title: Label-free detection of hemoglobin using GSH-AuAg NPs as fluorescent probe by dual quenching mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dongning
Yan, Jilin
Fang, Chen
Tu, Yifeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a glutathione-induced AuAg nanoparticles (GSH-AuAg NPs) was simply prepared with good fluorescent performance attributed to aggregation induced emission (AIE). Then an accurate fluorescent analytical strategy for label-free detection of hemoglobin (Hb) is presented based on its quenching effect for this fluorescence. The effect of Ag doping in nanoparticle on its fluorescent property is discussed in details, thereafter the quenching effect composed of two different mechanisms as redox and inner filtering effect is proposed. To make full effects of the Hb for quenching the fluorescence, the conditions for material preparation and Hb detection are optimized. This method achieves wide quantitative range for Hb detection from 1 nM to 3.5 μM with a detection limit of 0.58 nM, and was applied in the determination of Hb concentration in real blood samples successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hemoglobin
AuAg alloy
Glutathione-induced nanoparticle
Fluorescent probe
Quenching effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent GSH-AuAg NPs
The structural and FL property of prepared NPs
The FL quenching of GSH AuAg NPs by Hb
The detection performance of FL probe for Hb
Hb evaluation in real blood samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The (A) routine and (B) high-resolution TEM images of GSH-AuAg NPs, inset in (A) is the corresponding size distribution. (C) TEM images of GSH-Au NPs, inserted is the corresponding size distribution. (D) The excitation spectrum of GSH-AuAg NPs (black, λem = 583 nm), GSH-Au NPs (red, λem = 610 nm) and the emission spectrum of GSH-AuAg NPs (blue, λex = 290 nm), GSH-Au NPs (cyan, λex = 299 nm). Inserted are the photographs of GSH-AuAg NPs (left) and GSH-Au NPs (right) under UV lamp. (E) The FL spectra of GSH-AuAg NPs prepared with different feeding ratio of HAuCl4 to AgNO3, inset is the EDS spectrum of GSH-AuAg NPs prepared with feeding rate of 9:1. (F) The fluorescence decay curves of GSH-AuAg NPs and GSH-Au NPs aqueous solutions.

The (A) survey, (B, C) high-resolution Au4f and Ag3d XPS spectra of GSH-AuAg NPs. (D) The FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NPs prepared with different reaction time. (E) The CV curves of HAuCl4, AgNO3 and HAuCl4 + AgNO3 on indium tin oxide coated glass electrode.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of GSH-AuAg NPs, Hb and GSH-AuAg NPs + Hb. (B) The change of FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NPs + 1 × 10−6 M of Hb at different excitation wavelength. (C) The FL spectra of GSH-AuAg NPs (blank) in 5 × 10−6 M of Hb, heme, Cyt c, or 5 × 10−4 M of Fe2+, Fe3+. (D) The FL spectra of GSH-AuAg NPs, mixed with 0.5 mM of Fe2+ or Fe3+, even plus 5 mM of H2O2. (E) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Hb, Hb + H2O2, Hb + H2O2 + GSH-AuAg NPs. (F) The influence of H2O2 on GSH-AuAg NPs FL intensity at different Hb concentration.

(A) The effect of acidity of testing medium on GSH-AuAg NPs FL intensity. (B) The FL of GSH-AuAg NPs at different concentration of Hb. (C) The linear relationship between relative quenching degree and logCHb (1–1500 nM). (D) The linear relationship between relative quenching degree and CHb (1500–3500 nM). (E) The anti-interference capacity of the probe, CHb = 5 × 10−7 M, while others are 5 × 10−4 M. (F) The stability of GSH-AuAg NPs FL during two months.
The quenching mechanism of GSH-AuAg NPs FL by Hb.
Comparison of recent methods for the determination of Hb.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: DPV: differential pulse voltammetry; CS: Chitosan; MGCE: magnetic glassy carbon electrode; Fc-ECG: Fc[CO-Glu-Cys-Gly-OH]; SPE: screen-printed electrode; CDs: carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Hb in real samples.

head.ce:title: Label-free detection of hemoglobin using GSH-AuAg NPs as fluorescent probe by dual quenching mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Material Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Material Science, Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Material Science, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Endocrinology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou 215004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Endocrinology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Endocrinology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, 215004, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a glutathione-induced AuAg nanoparticles (GSH-AuAg NPs) was simply prepared with good fluorescent performance attributed to aggregation induced emission (AIE). Then an accurate fluorescent analytical strategy for label-free detection of hemoglobin (Hb) is presented based on its quenching effect for this fluorescence. The effect of Ag doping in nanoparticle on its fluorescent property is discussed in details, thereafter the quenching effect composed of two different mechanisms as redox and inner filtering effect is proposed. To make full effects of the Hb for quenching the fluorescence, the conditions for material preparation and Hb detection are optimized. This method achieves wide quantitative range for Hb detection from 1 nM to 3.5 μM with a detection limit of 0.58 nM, and was applied in the determination of Hb concentration in real blood samples successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemoglobin (Hb), an important protein component of erythrocyte, contains one non-covalently oxygen-binding heme group in each of its four polypeptide subunits (two α - and two β - globins) [1,2]. It plays a vital role for life to carry and transport oxygen to all of organs, meanwhile it will also bring carbon dioxide produced by the body from human tissues back to the lung and excrete it [3]. All of the Hb disorders no matter the concentration level or configuration will both raise the risk of organism imbalance, thus it acts an important diagnostic index for some diseases such as diabetes, iron-deficiency anemia, erythrocytosis, cardiovascular and heart diseases, etc [4]. Especially, except for those diseases related to oxygen metabolism, for type II diabetes mellitus (T2D), the ratio of glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) to Hb is a long-term index which reflects the time-averaged disease progression as a better diagnostic indicator beside blood glucose [5]. Therefore, the facile, accurate and rapid measurement of Hb in blood samples is significant for medical investigations.

For decades, various methods have already been reported for Hb detection. The molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) [6,7], antibody [8], adapter [9] were widely used to label Hb to achieve the specificity. However, the unique structure and special physicochemical properties make the label-free detection of Hb more feasible and possible for large-scale application [10]. For example, because of its electroactive, plenty of electrochemical Hb sensors have already been developed based on improved direct electron transfer by some redox intermediators like chitosan or methylene blue [11,12]. As well As, fluorometric detection is another optional alternative with the advantages as salient sensitivity, simplicity and so on because that ferrous heme gives Hb unique optical properties [13]. Semiconductor nanocrystals including quantum dots (QDs) and noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) have also been applied for Hb detection widely [14]. Li et al. synthesized hybrid Si QDs for fluorescent (FL) detection of Hb based on the coordination between imidazole (from silicon precursors) and Hb which give rise to inner filter effect (IFE) [15]. Lu et al. prepared polyethyleneimine protected Au8 nanoclusters and used it as a fluorescent probe, shows good response to Hb because of the quenching effect via the electron transfer between the hemin and Au(I) [16]. Also there are reports on sensitized Hb detection by H2O2 which could quench the fluorescence of Hb through Fenton reaction, built on the promoted H2O2 degradation by ferrous ion (Fe2+) in heme [17,18].

Recently, thiol-capped Au NPs exhibits excellent fluorescent sensibility due to aggregation induced emission (AIE) and has attracted broad attention [19,20]. Lots of sulfhydryl containing organics as proteins, polymers, peptides or nucleosides have been widely used as template to synthesis fluorescent Au NPs [21]. Among them, biomolecules have remarkable advantages of low toxicity, water-solubility, and good biocompatibility, show good performance in biological analysis fields like biosensors or cell imaging [14,22]. For example, Shalini has used BSA-Au NPs to label insulin antibody, found the recovery of Au NPs fluorescence by the specific immune reaction with insulin [23]. Yang has also employed BSA-Au NPs to act as a sensitive fluorescent probe for Hb detection, where H2O2 elevated the fluorescent quenching degree owing to the Fenton reaction [18]. Luo et al. have synthesized Au(0)@Au(I)–thiolate core–shell NPs, and invested the mechanism of AIE in detail [24]. They elucidated the origin of luminescence of thiolate-Au NCs, and affirmed the effect of glutathione (GSH) as reducing-protecting agent. However, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of most noble metal NPs is still not competitive to QDs or organic dye, which limits their application as more efficient fluorescent probe [25]. Recent studies have found, although the existence of Ag+ would induce photoluminescence inhibition as many other heavy metal ions, but slight doping of Ag into Au NCs has dramatic enhancing effect on FL emission, which is so-called “Ag effect” [26]. Zheng et al. prepared GSH-AuAg NPs possessing about 15-fold FL enhancement compared with single GSH-Au NPs [27]. Wang and coworkers used Ag to engineer the morphology of Au(I)-thiolate motifs in NP shell, got 3.9 times high luminescence intensity to pure Au NPs [28].

Herein, fluorescent AuAg NPs were synthesized taking GSH as template and reductant compatibly by a facile one-pot method, the “Ag effect” makes alloy NPs has about 7 times enhanced fluorescence compared to pure Au NPs. At 405 nm of exciting wavelength, Hb shows remarkable fluorescent quenching effect on GSH-AuAg NPs in two concentration intervals because of the decayed AIE or inner filtering effect, respectively. At pH 7.4, the prepared FL probe exhibits excellent accuracy to Hb detection in real blood samples.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2 30%), phosphates (NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Reduced L-glutathione (GSH) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich China (Shanghai, China). Hemoglobin (Hb) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Cytochrome C and hemin were obtained from Solarbio Technology Co., (Beijing, China). Hemoglobin HiCN test kit was purchased from JianCheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment. All chemicals and reagents are of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The fluorescence tests were performed on an F-2500 fluorospectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (PGeneral Instruments, China). Microscopic images of the nanoclusters were obtained with FEI Tecnai G20 and FEI Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. PLQY was measured by Quantaurus-QY Plus (Hamamatsu, Japan). FM-2015 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan) was used to measure PL lifetime. The chemical compositions and valence states were checked by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy on an XPS spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA) with Al Kα X-ray radiation (1486.6 eV) for excitation. Spectrophotometric spectrum was obtained by Infinite M200 Pro multimode reader (Tecan Infinite, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent GSH-AuAg NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent GSH-AuAg NPs were prepared based on a reported method with some modification [28]. 1.5 mL of 20 mM GSH, 0.90 mL of 20 mM HAuCl4, 0.10 mL of 20 mM AgNO3 were transferred into 7.5 mL of water successively, stirring (900 rpm) at 25 °C for 5 min, the mixture was heated to 85 °C and kept for 5 h. The pale yellow luminescent GSH-AuAg NPs colloid was formed, and was saved in shade at 4 °C for further use. The GSH-Au NPs and GSH-AuAg NPs with different Ag content were prepared by designed feeding rate of HAuCl4 to AgNO3, while other conditions keep the same.

Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.2 M) was used for controlling the acidity of detection medium. At 1 min after to add Hb into 20 times diluted GSH-AuAg NPs using PBS, the fluorescence spectra was measured under an excitation wavelength of 405 nm, set 20 nm of excitation and emission v band-slits.

For determining Hb in real samples, every 10 μL of blood were collected from volunteers or patients. Diluted by 90 μL of ultrapure water, waiting for 0.5 h to release Hb from red blood cells, it was then centrifuged for 15 min (1000 g, 4 °C). Then, 10 μL of supernate was transferred into 1 mL of PBS containing GSH-AuAg NPs for FL detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structural and FL property of prepared NPs
ce:section.ce:para: GSH-AuAg NPs is formed in slow co-reduction of Au and Ag precursors by GSH at hot environment. Fig. 1A shows its microtopography as uniformly distributed spherical NPs with the average diameter of 2.07 nm. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1B) reveals the distinct and uniform lattice fringes of prepared materials with inter-planar spacing distance of 0.22 nm, well matched with the (111) of AuAg alloy, a little smaller than GSH-Au NPs (Fig. 1C, particle size of 2.63 nm, and 0.25 nm of lattice spacing of Au (111) facet).

Fig. 1D is the fluorescent spectra of GSH-AuAg NPs and GSH-Au NPs. At 290 nm of the excitation wavelength, the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NPs gets to maximum at the emission wavelength of 583 nm. By comparison, the excitation wavelength of GSH-Au NPs is 299 nm and the emission wavelength is 610 nm. Clearly the “Ag effect” makes 27 nm of blue shift for emission wavelength and about 7 time higher FL intensity than pure Au NPs. To explore the effect of Ag content on the FL property of GSH-AuAg NPs, the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NPs prepared at different feeding ratio of HAuCl4 to AgNO3 (6:4, 7:3, 8:2, 9:1 and 95:5) was compared. As shown in Fig. 1E, when the Ag precursor accounts for 10%, i.e. 90.3–9.7 of atomic ratio (confirmed by energy dispersive spectrometry, EDS), GSH-AuAg NPs gives highest FL emission. The PL delay curves of GSH-AuAg NPs and GSH-Au NPs were recorded using a phosphorescence measurement manner (Fig. 1F). The average photoluminescence decay time of GSH-AuAg NPs is 2.39 ns, similar to that of GSH-Au NPs (2.51 ns). Significantly, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of GSH-AuAg NPs is 9%, three times to that of GSH-Au NPs (3%).

With XPS to investigate the elemental valence states on the particle surface [29], Fig. 2A shows the existence of C, S, N, O on the XPS survey spectra, confirming the GSH coating. Meanwhile, the high-resolution Au 4f spectrum of GSH-AuAg NPs (Fig. 2B) exhibited two strong diffraction peaks at 84.3 and 88.1 eV, different from 83.4 and 87.1 eV of Au (0), verifying Au (I) is the main form on the NPs surface. Similarly, the Ag 3d5/2 spectrum (Fig. 2C) was then divided into Ag (I) and Ag (0) forms with binding energies of 368.2 and 367.5 eV respectively, hereof Ag (I) is in majority.

Fig. 2D reflects the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NPs prepared underwent different reaction time, gets maximum with reaction time of 5 h. This implies that the formation of GSH-AuAg NPs is a slow process due to the weak reducibility of GSH. There the small clusters were formed by self-nucleation due to the aggregation of those Au and Ag atoms reduced from AuCl4 - and Ag+ by GSH in early stage, after that the size of nuclei kept growing by way of the deposition of atoms [30]. This is time-consuming process, short time does not meet the requirement to form a mature structure. But, too long time conversely leads to poor fluorescent behavior, because of the presence of overmuch Au (0) on particle surface, as known the fluorescence is significantly influenced by the ratio between Au (I) and Au (0) [31].

Fig. 2E shows the cyclic voltammograms (CV) of HAuCl4, AgNO3 and their mixture (both 2 mM). It is clear that the reduction of Ag+ happened later (0.68 V vs. SCE) than AuCl4 − (1.20 V vs. SCE) because of lower redox potential [32]. So, Ag+ mostly plays a role as linker to form larger and compacter Au(I)/Ag(I)–thiolate motifs on the surface of NPs [28,33]. Refer to reported AIE of Au(I)−thiolate complex using GSH to reduce Au(III) [24], and the highly fluorescent gold nanoclusters synthesized using bovine serum albumin (because it contains organic sulfurs) [34], the strong FL emission of GSH-AuAg NPs also can be attributed to AIE, for the high affinity of sulfhydryl of GSH to Au(I) and Ag(I) to form inter-/intra-complex cross-links. Wherein, the aurophilic bonds between Au(I)/Ag(I) further draws NPs compacted, which is the premise of cation-induced aggregation, then promoting FL [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FL quenching of GSH AuAg NPs by Hb
ce:section.ce:para: Refer to ascertained fluorescent mechanism of GSH-AuAg NPs [24], the main reason of quenching effect of Hb might rationally be attributed to ferroheme [35]. As for the specific mechanism, the inner filtering effect (IFE) must be an important role. It means the attenuation of fluorescence due to absorption of excitation or emission light by the large concentrations of light-absorbing substances in the system. The IFE generally occurs when the absorption band of the absorber possesses complementary overlap with the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore [36]. Several recent studies have applied the IFE in developing fluorescent probes although it is regarded as a source of errors in fluorimetry at first [37,38]. As shown in Fig. 3A, Hb has a broad peak at deep ultraviolet region (280 nm) and a narrow absorption peak at visible region (405 nm), but the addition of GSH-AuAg NPs did not cause the change of the wavelength of these absorption peaks, means there was not allosteric alteration on Hb structure. Fig. 3B displays the rate of FL change of GSH-AuAg NPs in the presence or absence of 5 × 10−6 M of Hb upon different excitation wavelength to further explore the filtering effect of Hb. Maximally, up to about 93% of lost FL intensity was occurred at excitation wavelength of 405 nm, obviously larger than other excitation wavelength, give a full play of Hb IFE because 405 nm is its absorption peak wavelength.

Fig. 3C displays the different degree of FL quenching efficiency of some blood components as Hb, cytochrome c (Cyt c) and heme. The results give an order of Hb > heme > Cyt c, here Hb shows the strongest quenching effect because each Hb molecule contains four heme subgroups [35]. Of course the influence of Cyt c to the detection of Hb is negligible because of its low concentration (1.6 nM) in blood than one in a million of Hb (1.8 mM). The irons also have quenching effect, the FL intensity was quenched for 33.8% with 5 × 10−4 M of Fe3+, bigger than that of Fe2+ (22.3%), consist with the reported results [37]. It is believable that the electron transfer between Fe3+ and excited-state of GSH-AuAg NPs led to strong fluorescence quenching, and the Fe3+ also had higher affinity to N or O atoms of GSH, reducing the aggregation degree of GSH-AuAg NPs [39].

The quenching mechanism of Hb for the FL of GSH-AuAg NPs also can be further elucidated by the influence of H2O2. H2O2 would react with Fe2+ to produce oxidative hydroxyl or hydroperoxyl radicals, called “Fenton reaction”. These strong oxidative radicals will destroy the stability of NPs [40], thus to obviously suppress the FL intensity when H2O2 was added into solution together with Fe2+ (Fig. 3D). Meanwhile, the ∙OH free radicals could also destroy the porphyrin structure of Hb at certain positions in short distance, led to a looser structure [41,42]. So, as shown in Fig. 3E, there is a significantly weakened absorbance of Hb with 10 nm of wavelength redshift after H2O2 addition. The reason for this is it would reduce the inner filtering effect of Hb on FL of GSH-AuAg NPs. As a result, the addition of H2O2 led to the decrease of FL intensity at low Hb concentration, but the opposite results in higher concentrated Hb (typically 1.5–2.5 μM) because the IFE is dominant right now (Fig. 3F).

Based on the above discussion, the quenching mechanism of Hb on the FL of GSH-AuAg NPs can be illustrated using Scheme 1. Firstly, the aurophilic interaction of the Au(I)/Ag(I) on the particles surface was reduced by the electron transfer between Hb and NPs, leading to looser distribution of NPs. This finally attenuated the FL intensity due to depressed AIE. Meanwhile, the IFE of Hb at higher concentration is another main mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection performance of FL probe for Hb
ce:section.ce:para: The suitable pH condition for Hb detection was explored, as shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescent intensity decreased along with the pH from 7.0 to 10.0, considering that the pH of human blood is 7.4, the PBS (pH=7.4) was selected. The fluorescent intensity of GSH-AuAg NPs decreased along with added concentration of Hb (1–3500 nM) as shown in Fig. 4B. The relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and the CHb gives two entities of linearity in different concentration range because of different primary quenching mechanism. In the range from 1 nM to 1.5 μM, the relative fluorescent quenching displays a good linear regression (R2 = 0.996) against the log (CHb) as y = 0.212 logCHb − 0.0112 with the limit of detection (LOD) for 0.58 nM (S/N = 3. Fig. 4 C). Another linear equation is y = 0.105 CHb + 0.500 (R2 =0.995) in the concentration range of 1.5–3.5 μM (Fig. 4D), when IFE takes a dominant position in FL quenching. The performance is comparable to recent Hb determination methods as listed in Table 1.

In order to verify the feasibility of this fluorescent probe in practical application, potential interferents were tested to check the selectivity of this FL method. The results show less than 10% of quenched fluorescent intensity at each 5 × 10−4 M of metal cation (Na+, Mg2+, K+), bio-molecules (AA, DA, Glu, Ins) and proteins (albumin, low-density lipoprotein and myosinogen), see Fig. 4E. All these exhibit the good tolerance of prepared FL probe to common blood components. Meanwhile, the FL probe shows excellent stability stored at 4 °C in dark place, the FL performance almost has no change after 2 months (Fig. 4F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hb evaluation in real blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical applicability of present GSH-AuAg NPs FL probe, the concentration of Hb in blood samples of healthy volunteers and diabetes patients were detected (listed in Table 2). Those results of Hb in finger blood of healthy peoples (sample 1–3) were verified by HiCN method [44], and the results of those venous blood samples of diabetes patient (sample 4–6) were checked with hospital standard (colorimetric method). The detection results of the proposed FL method are in good agreement with the standard method, and obtained acceptable recovery from 99.1% to 104%.

In conclusion, fluorescent GSH-AuAg NPs were prepared using GSH as template and reductant by a simple one-pot aqueous method. The doping of Ag makes alloy NPs have promoted fluorescence property based on the AIE and “Ag effect”. Using exciting wavelength of 405 nm, the emission at 583 nm of this NP probe was quenched intensively by Hb based on the electron transfer between NPs and Hb, and the inner filtering effect of Hb at higher concentration. This dual quenching effect endows with low LOD of 0.58 nM and wide detection range (from 1 to 3500 nM) for Hb detection. The real sample detection results of this easy-operated and fast FL method were correlated with those obtained by standard method, revealing its great potential application for the monitoring of Hb concentration in blood.

Dongning Li: Methodology, Experiment, Data analysis, Writing – original draft. Jilin Yan: Experimental verification, Partly participate in material preparation. Chen Fang: Research objective initiative, Investigation, Specimen collection. Yifeng Tu: Project instructor, Project design and Methodology, Experimental verification, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675115, 21375091).

Bioelectron., 142 (2019) 111515.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Dongning is now a Ph.D. candidate in analytical chemistry at Soochow University. He got his Bachelor's degree at 2015 and master’s degree at 2018 in Suzhou University of Science and Technology. His main research interest is the preparation and application of biosensors for clinical diagnosis.

Yan Jilin is a vice-professor in Soochow University, he received his Ph.D. degree in 2006 from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, and worked as postdoctoral fellow in Department of Chemistry, University of Florida from 2006 to 2008. His research interest is focused on sensing technology based on molecular recognition and luminescent detection.

Fang Chen is an associate chief physician in Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University. He achieved his Ph.D. degree from Soochow University in 2019 under the supervision of Prof. Tu Yifeng. He has rich clinical experience in diagnosis, accurate classification and therapy of diabetes.

Tu Yifeng is a professor of Soochow University, doctoral supervisor. He received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2001. His research area is chemically modified electrode, biosensor and bioelectrochemistry, electrochemi/bioelectrochemi-luminescence for diagnosis and early warning of series diseases.
